title_tesim: The O'Moyne Family, cast members in a scene from Sally and Saint Anne
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: From: U-I, Universal City, Calif. "Sally and St. Anne"--- A Pictorial Synopsis"Sally and St. Anne" is a comedy that is both human and spiritual, hearty and soulful, dealing with the wonder workings of a faith as Irish as leprechauns. Starring Ann Blyth and Edmund Gwenn, it was written by James O'Hanlon and Herb Meadow, directed by Rudy Maté and produced by Leonard Goldstein for Universal-International. The story in pictures:3. Sally lives in a home that is humble, but honest, and subject to the elations and despair familiar to the Irish. Her brother Willie (Lamont Johnson) is an aspiring composer who can't compose. Brother Mike (Jack Kelly) is "The Great O'Moyne" who dreams of being a great magician. Brother Danny (Hugh O'Brian) is a fighter who gets knocked out every fight. Pop (Otto Hulett) and Mom (Frances Bavier) have their hands full keeping the kids on even keel - and making both ends meet.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Cast member prepares for battle on the set of Guerrilla Girl
caption_tesim: Helmut Dantine in "Guerrilla Girl" introducing MariannaProduced and directed by John ChristianReleased thru United Artists
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: Excerpted from the AFI listing:"Guerrilla Girl" marked Greek producer-director John Christian's first American feature release. According to a Los Angeles Times news item, the actress billed as Marianna was working as Christian's production secretary before being cast in the film. A modern source lists the working title as "Dawn Comes Late."

In this photograph taken from a scene not found in the final cut of the film, "Guerrilla Girl," it is difficult to identify the actress in the photo. Although wearing a similar costume, it does not appear to be the credited lead actress, Marianna. Other photographs from the film within this collection, as well as the film itself, were used for comparison. Perhaps she is a stunt double or simply a completely different and, unfortunately, unidentified and uncredited actress in the film.

Greek film producer/director, Giannis Hristodoulou, was credited in the film, "Guerrilla Girl" as, John Christian. He has also been credited in other films as, Jan Christian and Ioannis Hristodoulou, as well as by his birth name, Giannis Hristodoulou.
AFI lists a release date and copyright date for the film, "Guerrilla Girl,' as January 23, 1953.

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen and Richard Todd in London after filming Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Vera-Ellen Sees London With Richard Todd. During the filming of Happy-Go-Lovely at Elstree studios, Hollywood dancing star Vera-Ellen had no chance of looking around London. She was either rehearsing or before the cameras every day of the production. So when at last, after five months hard work, her part in the film was completed Vera-Ellen decided to see the famous landmarks of England's capital for herself. Her guide and companion for the day was Britain's top male star, Richard (Hasty Heart) Todd, who was himself sightseeing in Hollywood only a few months earlier. Together, the two made a lightning tour of the city, visiting such well-known spots as Picadilly Circus, Trafalgar Square, Fleet Street, Green Park and the Houses of Parliament. Richard Todd and Vera-Ellen pause near Green Park to buy fruit from a typical London costermonger.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen, Diane Hart, Cesar Romero, David Niven, and extras in Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Theatrical producer John Frost (Cesar Romero) gives an order at the Bon Viveur Restaurant, Edinburgh. Frost is dining with Janet Jones (Vera-Ellen), B.G. Bruno (David Niven) and Mae Thompson (Diane Hart). Bruno, a millionaire manufacturer, is a man of simple tasted and does not appreciate the cigar Frost has given him. Frost: Now, what shall we have to start with? How about some caviare?. Bruno: Not for me, thanks. I think it's very overrated and much too expensive. A scene from Happy-Go-Lovely, the Associated British-Marcel Hellman Technicolor production starring David Niven, Vera-Ellen and Cesar Romero with Bobby Howes and Diane Hart.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

Bruno is talking, his secretary passes across an envelope to while a white gardenia is pinned. Bruno: What's this?. Mr. Dodds (uncomfortably): Er... it seems to be some sort of a bill, sir....A scene from Happy-Go-Lovely, the Marcel Hellman/Associated British production starring Vera-Ellen, David Niven and Cesar Romero. title_tesim: John Litel, Ida Lupino and Stephen McNally in a scene from Woman in Hiding
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: "Woman In Hiding" -- A Pictorial Synopsis; "Woman In Hiding" is melodrama with the excitement of a chase and a highly original story premise: a woman discovers on her wedding night that she is the intended murder victim of her bridegroom! Produced by Universal-International, it stars Ida Lupino, Stephen McNally, Howard Duff and introduces Peggy Dow, new dramatic sensation who was assigned this second feminine lead as her first movie role. Written by Oscar Saul, the film is directed by Michael Gordon and produced by Michel Kraike. The story in pictures: 1. Selden Clark (Stephen McNally), member of an aristocratic family in the southern town of Clarksville, is general manager of a mill owned by John Chandler (John Litel). Coveting control of the mill, Clark pays court to Chandler's daughter, Deborah (Ida Lupino). She would like to marry Clark, but has delayed because she senses her father's distrust for her fiance.

title_tesim: Dana Andrews, Benny Baker, George Mathews, and Danny Kaye in Up In Arms
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph:SF-29 and 26.TROUBLE IN THE BARRACKS -- Danny Kaye, as a new recruit in the Army, is at the mercy of Benny Baker and George Mathews, his tough sergeant, but Dana Andrews is coming up in the rear to intercede in the scene from Samuel Goldwyn's "Up In Arms," Technicolor comedy with music. Danny, starring as a comic hypochondriac in the picture, is striggling [sp] against drinking a concoction that has been mixed up from his various and sundry medicines. It is punishment for playing the sergeant's victrola without permission. Danny Kaye, the noted Broadway comedian, makes his screen debut in the offering which also introduces Constance Dowling, of the New York stage, and features Dinah Shore. Elliott Nugent directs.

title_tesim: James Mason and Deborah Kerr in Hatter's Castle
caption_tesim: Robert Newton, James Mason, Deborah Kerr, Emlyn Williams inHatter's Castle.A Paramount Picture
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: From the AFI listing: "Hatter's Castle" was produced by Paramount's British company in 1941, but was not released in the United States until 1948. According to the Los Angeles Times, the picture was held back until its stars were better known to American audiences.
The British film, "Hatter's Castle," released in Great Britain on November 12, 1941, was released in the U.S.A. as "A. J. Cronin's Hatter's Castle," on June 18, 1948.
From the AFI listing: Based on the novel "Hatter's Castle," by A. J. Cronin (Boston, 1931)

Producer, Isadore Goldsmith, credited in the film, "Hatter's Castle," as I. Goldsmith, has also been credited as producer for other films as, I. G. Goldsmith, Isadore G. Goldsmith and Isidore Goldschmidt.

title_tesim: Will Rogers as Lem Skagwillow in The Golden Age of Comedy
caption_tesim: "The Golden Age of Comedy" Starring Will Rogers - Carole Lombard - Laurel and Hardy - Jean Harlow - Harry Langdon - Ben TurpinProduced by Robert Youngson
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: The 1958 film, "The Golden Age of Comedy," chronicles the variety of comedic styles and talent at the height of Hollywood's silent era. The film was culled from 2,000 reels of Mack Sennett and Hal Roach short film comedies and divided into eight acts with a prologue and finale. This photograph was taken from a scene from the 1923 silent film, "Uncensored Movies," starring Will Rogers.
